content.full_text:
Re
Farn Polrucal PnacrrcEs CourralssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
April 19,2005
Peter A. Laurence, Councilman
Better Homes Realty
l5l I Treat Blvd., Ste. 100
Walnut Creek, CA 94598
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-0t045
Dear Mr. Laurence:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act')-r Nothing in this letter should
be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter
is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission') does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby
(r97s) I FPPC ops. 71.)
QUESTION
Are you prohibited under the Act's conflict of interest provisions from
participating in any governmental decisions regarding the city's amendments to the rules
governing collections of business licensing fees and penalties?
CONCLUSION
No. Under the Act's conflict of interest provisions, the govemmental decisions
relating to changes in the city's collection practices for business licensing fees and
penalties for businesses located outside of Clalon would not have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of your economic interests, as you
have presented the facts, and you may participate in the decisions.
I Government Code sectiorx 81000 - 9l}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. 4-05-045
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are a member of the city council for the City of Clayton and arcal estate
broker in Walnut Creek, California. You are licensed by the state, and you pay local
business tax where your office is located. You also sell property in many cities
throughout Northern californi4 including some within clayton's city limit.
The city council hired a company, Municipal Auditing Services (MAS), to help
enforce Clayton's business license tax collection, based upon your 1978 Ordinance. The
city gets 55 percent of what is collected and MAS gets 45 percent. As part of its
collection practices, MAS is seeking to require businesses located outside Clayton, but
who do any business within Cla5rton, to become licensed, and they me charging such
businesses penalties for anypast years in which they did business in Clayton without first
obtaining a business license. As a real estate broker who has sold homes within Clayton,
you af,e one of those businesses now subject to such licensing fees and penalties.
As a result of the above actions by MAS, the city council is now re-evaluating its
policies on business license enforcement, and is considering either terminating its
agreement with MAS or amending the agreement to somehow lessen the impact of these
requirements on business located outside the dishict that do business in Clayton The
city attorney has advised you not to participate in any discussions or votes on the
business license tax issue, so you have removed yourself from any involvement and have
not attended three meetings so far on this topic.
You are one of seven equal shareholders in Better Homes Realty, the business
under which you operate as a real estate broker. During the last fiscal year the business
had net income of less than $500,000 and earning before taxes of less than $750,000
including income from sales you made. You have received income frorn the business
aggregating more than $500 in the last 12 months.
You have indicated that MAS is attempting to collect three years past licensing
fees and penalties amounting to $1,826 against you- In a subsequent conversation, you
stated that the $1,826 amount was being chmged based on your California real estate
brokers' license on the basis of home sales in Clayton during that period in which you
have represented one of the parties. No business license fees are otherwise being sought
from your business, Better Homes Realty.
ANALYSIS
PorrNrmr, Coxrr,rcr oF INTEREsT
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported thern." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making or


File No. 4-05-045
Page No. 3
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifring conflict of interest. (Regulation 187000).) The general
rule is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his
or her financial interests.
Srprs I & 2: Anr You A Pust,rc Orrrcr,q,L Mlxrxc, P*rrcparrNc rN MAKTNG, oR
Ixrr,unxcrNc A GovsnxurENTAL Drcrsror?
As a member of the Clalon City Council, you me a public official under the Act.
(Section 82048.) You will be called upon to consider whether the city should amend an
existing contract with an outside agency related to the city collection practices for business
licenses and penalties. Therefore, you will be making participating in making or
otherrriseusing his official position to influence a govemmental decision.
Srnp 3: Do You Ilave a PorrxrrALLY Drsqulr,rrvrxc ECoNoMIC INTEREST?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generall5 on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investrnentof $2,000ormore(g 87103(a);regulation 18703.1(a)); orinwhichheor
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employeg or holds any position of
management ($ 87 I 03 (d); regulation I 8703. I (b));
a
a
a
o
a
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more ($ 87103(b); regulation 187O3.2);
An economic interest in any source of incomg including promisod income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (g 87103(c);
regulation 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him orher ifthe gifts aggregate to $360
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (g 87103(e); regulation 18703.4);
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family - this is the "personal financial effects" rule
($ 87103; regulation 18703.5).


File No. A-05-045
Page No. 4
Under the facts you have presented, you have an economic interest in the business
entity through which you operate as a real estate broker as a result of your investment in
and source of income through the business entity.
You also have an economic interest in any client of the business who has provided
you a pro-rata share (i.e., one-seventh share) of income aggregating $500 or more to you
during the l2-month period prior to the time the governmental decision is made. You
have not provided us with sufficient information to analyze this economic interest any
firther.
Finally, since you have indicated that the fees are being assessed against you and
you have stated that they will be paid by you from your own personal funds, this suggests
that the decision in question may have a personal financial effect on you apart from
effects on your other economic interests.
Srrp 4: Ann Youn EcoNomrc Ixrnnusrs Drnrcrr,y m INDTRECTLy IIwoLvED rN
THE GOYERNMDNTAL DTCTSTCXV ?
"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision." (Regulation I 870a(a).)
Business Entities and Sources of Income: For govemmental decisions that
affect business entities and sources of incomg the standmds set forth in regulation
18704.1(a) apply.
Regulation 18704.I (a) states:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income ... is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person, either directly or by agent:
(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the zubject o{ the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person."
Since it does not appear that your economic interest falls under the standards set
forth in regulation 1870a)@), your economic interests, both with respect to Better


File No. A-05-045
Page No. 5
Homes Realty and your individual business activities as a licensed broker, would be
indirectly involved.
In addition, regulation 18704.5 provides
"(a) A public official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be
directly involved in a governmental decision which has any financial
effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her irnmediate
family."
Srpp 5: MartnranrrySrANDARD
Source of IncomelBusiness Entity: Regulation 1S705.3OX1) states that for
indirectly involved sources of income that are business entities, the materiality standards set
forth in regulation 18705- 1(c) apply. Your facts indicate that for its most recent fiscal year,
Better Homes Realty's net income was less than $5fi),000 and its net earnings before taxes
was less than $750,000. Since any business income you made as an individual broker is
included in the income and earnings of Better Homes Realty, income and earning for you as
an individual broker would, necessarily also be less than these amounts. As a result, the
materiality standard contained in regulation 18705.1(c)(4) applies. Under regulation
18705.1(c)(4), the financial effect of a governmental decision on arelatively small business
in which a public official has an interest and which is indirectly involved in the governmental
decision is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
' 
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or,
(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."
Personal Financial Effect: Please note that, as discussed abovg you may have
an economic interest under the "personal financial effects" rule. Regulation 1S705.5(b)
provides:
"The financial effects of a decision which affects only the salary, per
diem, or reimbursernent for expenses the public official or a member of
his or her immediate family receives from a federal, state, or local
government agency shall not be deerned material, unless the decision is to


File No. A-05-045
Page No. 6
hire, fire, promote, demote, suspend without pay or otherwise take
disciplinary action with financial sanction against the official or a member
of his or her immediate family, or to set a salary for the official or a
member of his or her immediate family which is different from salaries
paid to other employees of the government agency in the same job
classifi cation or position. "
Because the fee is based on operation of yourbusiness in the jurisdiction, the
exception in regulation 18705.5(b) applies, we do not finther analyzethis economic
interest.
Srrr 6: Rrasor*ABly FoRrSEEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a govemmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding thedecision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeablg but it must be more'than a mere
possibility. (In re Ihorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 19S.)
Under the facts you have presented, the reasonable foreseeable financial effect of
the govemmental decisions may^result, at most, in the business entity avoiding the
additional expense of the 91,8262 business license and late penalties fees, whiih the
goveflrmental decisions would either lessen or negate forbusinesses located outside of
Clayton.
Accordingly provided the govemmental decisions result in a reduction or
elimination of expenses existing expense ofno greaterthan 91,826, and that is less than
the materiality standard of $5,000 set forth in regulation 18705.1(c)(a)(@), the
goveflImental decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interests. Thereforg you do not have a conflict of interest in
participating in this decision.
STnps 7 & 8: PusLTc GTxnn,c.LLY & LEGALLY REQIIIRED PARTICIPATIoN
The "public generally''and "legally rquired participation" exceptions allow,
under certain circumstances, public officials to participate in a govemmantal decision
even if they are otherwise deemed to have a conflict of interest under the Act. Although
2 Our analysis ilssumes that this amount represents the total amount of licensing fees and penalties
potentially due from your business interests, both as an individual broker and from Better Homes Realty. If
there are additional business licensing fees athributed to Better Homes based on, for example, fees on the
real estate licenses of other individuals working for the business who also work within the jurisdiction,
these amounts could affect the materiality determination made herein. For example if the total amount of
fees due reaches $5,000 or more as a result ofthe governmental decisions you could have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests, and you would be prohibited from
participating.


File No. 4-05-045
Page No. 7
you have asked for assistance in determining if the public generally exception applies to
your situation, since we have found that you do not have a conflict of interest under the
Act, there is no need for us to analyze this exception or the legally required participation
exception.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: William J. Lenkeit
Counsel, Legal Division
WJL:rd
I:\Adviceltrs\05-045

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are you prohibited under the Act's conflict of interest provisions from
participating in any governmental decisions regarding the city's amendments to the rules
governing collections of business licensing fees and penalties?

CONCLUSION: No. Under the Act's conflict of interest provisions, the govemmental decisions
relating to changes in the city's collection practices for business licensing fees and
penalties for businesses located outside of Clalon would not have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of your economic interests, as you
have presented the facts, and you may participate in the decisions.
